1: Psalm 125 is a psalm of trust using the physical geography of Jerusalem as its controlling metaphor. "Those who trust in the LORD (habotehim baYHWH) are as firm as Mount Zion — unmovable, enduring forever" — the theology of trust is expressed through the geology of the city. Mount Zion had become, for Israelite theology, the symbol of divine stability — not because of the mountain's size (it is modest by world standards) but because of what YHWH had chosen to do there. The covenant promise of Ps 132:14 ("this is my resting place forever") made Zion theologically immovable regardless of its physical vulnerability. To trust in YHWH is to have the stability of the mountain that YHWH has claimed: unshakeable not because of inherent strength but because of the divine commitment that underwrites it.
2: "Just as the mountains encircle Jerusalem (harim saviv li'rushalayim), so the LORD encircles his people, now and forever" — the physical topography of Jerusalem provides the metaphor: the city sits in a basin surrounded by the Mount of Olives to the east, Mount Scopus to the northeast, and higher ground on the west and south. A traveler arriving at Jerusalem found the city visually enclosed by hills on all sides — a natural geographic encirclement that the psalm transforms into a theological image. As the hills surround the city providing natural shelter, YHWH surrounds his people with divine protection that is permanent ("now and forever"). The landscape the pilgrim walks through becomes a living parable of divine guardianship: even the topography preaches the gospel of YHWH's encircling care.
3: "The scepter of the wicked will not be allowed to remain (lo yanuah) over the territory given to the righteous, lest the righteous be tempted to do wrong" — the political-moral concern: foreign domination (the "scepter of the wicked") over covenant land creates an ongoing moral hazard for the righteous. If wicked rulers govern long enough, the righteous will be tempted to accommodate, collaborate, or imitate their methods — to survive by becoming like the oppressor. The psalm names this moral danger explicitly: the duration of wicked rule matters, not just its fact. YHWH's covenant promise includes the guarantee that wicked rule over the righteous's territory will not be permanent — a promise that sustained Jewish hope through Babylonian, Persian, Seleucid, and Roman domination and continues to frame the theology of political oppression.
4: "Bless those who are good (tovim), O LORD, and all who are upright in heart (yesharim belibbotam)" — the petition for blessing upon the covenant faithful is the positive counterpart to the implied judgment on the wicked. The pairing of "good" (tov) and "upright" (yashar) identifies both external behavior and internal orientation: the good person does good; the upright person has an inner alignment with divine standards. "In heart (belibbotam)" — the heart as the seat of intention and moral orientation (Jer 17:9-10; Ps 51:10; 1 Sam 16:7: "the LORD looks at the heart"). The blessing petition assumes that YHWH distinguishes between those whose uprightness is external performance and those whose hearts are genuinely aligned with the covenant values — and blesses the latter.
5: "But as for those who swerve onto crooked paths (hamattim aqalqallotam), the LORD will banish them along with those who do evil. Peace be upon Israel (shalom al-Yisra'el)" — those who "swerve" (natah, to turn aside, to deviate) onto "crooked paths" (aqalqallot, twisted, winding, devious routes) are those who deviate from the straight covenant path while maintaining the outward appearance of covenant community membership. The wisdom tradition consistently contrasts the straight path (derek yashar) with the crooked path of the wicked (Prov 2:15; 4:26-27; 11:20; 28:6, 10). "Peace be upon Israel" is the closing liturgical blessing — the same formula ends Ps 128:6 and is applied to the church in Gal 6:16. The Songs of Ascents consistently close with this covenant-community blessing, connecting the individual pilgrim's journey to the communal shalom that is the goal of all pilgrimage.
